title: Dog Arched Back and Walking Stiffly: Causes and Urgent Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your dog has an arched back and stiff gait. Understand the urgency of spinal pain and how to triage these symptoms effectively.
content_html:
TL;DR: An arched back and stiff gait are signs of severe pain or spinal issues and should be treated as a veterinary emergency to avoid permanent damage. Capturing video of the behavior at home can significantly assist your veterinarian in providing an accurate triage and diagnosis.

What does it mean if my dog has an arched back and a stiff gait?

When a dog arches their back (a posture known as kyphosis) and walks with stiff, guarded movements, it is a clear physical manifestation of significant pain. This posture is often a protective mechanism where the dog attempts to stabilize their spine or guard a painful abdomen. Common causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), muscle spasms, or inflammatory conditions like pancreatitis.

Is an arched back and stiff walking a veterinary emergency for my dog?

  • Urgency Level: High. An arched back coupled with stiff walking is considered a veterinary emergency, especially in high-risk breeds like Dachshunds, Corgis, and French Bulldogs.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if your dog shows signs of weakness, is dragging their toes, or is unable to stand.
  • Do not delay treatment, as spinal issues can quickly lead to permanent neurological damage or paralysis.

How can photos or videos help the veterinarian triage my dog's condition?

  • Capture a brief video of your dog walking or a clear photo of their arched posture before leaving for the clinic.
  • Documenting behavior at home is vital because adrenaline can temporarily mask a dog's pain or stiffness during a clinical exam.
  • Sharing a visual record helps the medical team accurately assess the severity of the gait abnormality and prioritize your pet's care.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Lumbosacral junction abnormalities, detectable via ultrasonography, can cause back pain. These include congenital issues like lumbosacral ankylosis or intervertebral ankylosis between L5 and L6, disc degenerative lesions affecting the lumbosacral disc (fissuration, cavitation, mineralization, ventral herniation), intervertebral malalignment (spondylolisthesis) of the lumbosacral joint or the joint between L5 and L6, and intertransverse lumbosacral osteoarthrosis. Acute and severe strain of the sacroiliac ligaments, often with a history of injury, can cause severe pain in the pelvic or sacroiliac region, often with marked hindlimb lameness.

Chapter: Neurology, Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1147)

faqs.question: Should I encourage my dog to walk if they are stiff?
faqs.answer: No, you should strictly limit their movement. Keep them in a small crate or on a short leash and avoid any jumping or stair climbing until they are evaluated by a vet.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog over-the-counter pain relief?
faqs.answer: Never provide human medications like Ibuprofen or Aspirin, as these can be toxic to dogs and may interfere with the medications your vet needs to prescribe.

faqs.question: What are other warning signs to watch for?
faqs.answer: Look for shivering, loss of appetite, reluctance to lift the head, or 'knuckling' (walking on the tops of the paws).
